Living Die: Necropolitics, Patriarchal Stigma, and the Performance of Late Capital Resistance

A Visually Allegory that maps the zombie-land of algorithmic sovereignty onto the scaffold of gendered punishment - refusing catharsis, insisting on witness
SH
Prof. S. M. Alfarid Hussain, Department of Mass Communication 4 April 2026

Living Die — A Drama Performance by the Department of Visual Arts, Assam University, Silchar

A Review by Prof. S. M. Alfarid Hussain

‘Living Die’ a visual presentation by the Department of Visual Arts presents itself as a layered visual allegory, operating simultaneously at the macro-political and the micro-gendered registers of contemporary society. On one level, the performance stages a Trumpian dystopia, a zombie-land of the “living dead” governed by political oligarchs and big tech moguls. On another, it evokes the spectral figure of Hester Prynne from Nathaniel Hawthorne’s ‘The Scarlet Letter,’ reanimating her as an archetype of gendered punishment and defiant speech within a patriarchal order. The critical force of the work lies precisely in this juxtaposition, the undead citizenry of global necropolitics and the stigmatised female body are revealed as twin expressions of the same structural logic, the violent production of disposable subjects who nonetheless persist in futile but irrepressible rebellion.

Drawing on Achille Mbembe’s concept of necropolitics, ‘Living Die’ depicts a populace reduced to Giorgio Agamben’s “bare life,” biologically alive but symbolically and civically dead. The zombie here is not a supernatural monster but a metaphor for late-capitalist subjectivity under algorithmic governance. Political oligarchs and tech moguls function as contemporary sovereigns, manipulating collective psyche through what Bernard Stiegler terms the systematic destruction of psychic and collective individuation. The human subjects’ resistance, while defiant, remains trapped in what Judith Butler identifies as the psychic life of power, a loop where submission to structural violence becomes internalised even as it is resisted. The performance thus critiques the double bind of global citizenship, the impossibility of escaping techno-feudal control and the tragic persistence of rebellion that, though ineffective, attests to a residual desire for authenticity.

The performance restages the public scaffold as a Foucauldian disciplinary spectacle, where a male chauvinistic and patriarchal society imposes what Erving Goffman terms a ‘spoiled identity,’ a scarlet letter of stigma to silence and humiliate. Yet, echoing Kate Millett and Simone de Beauvoir, the woman’s defiance is not erased but marked. Her “rightful place” is precisely the centre from which patriarchal logic seeks to expel her. The performance thus recasts Hester as a prototype of political speech, her body becoming a site of both punishment and testimony. In alignment with feminist performance artists from Carolee Schneemann to #MeToo protest works, ‘Living Die’ reclaims public shame as a tool for critique rather than submission.

Ultimately, ‘Living Die’ offers no redemptive resolution. It stages a global citizenry ensnared in a double bind, unable to escape political and structural violence, yet unwilling to abandon rebellion. The Hester Prynne figure does not triumph, she persists. The zombies do not revolt, they endure. In this refusal of catharsis lies the work’s critical power. It bears witness to what Butler might call the grievability of those rendered socially dead, and to what Hawthorne knew, that the scaffold, whether Puritan or algorithmic, never fully extinguishes the voice it seeks to punish. ‘Living Die’ is thus a vital contribution to visual critique, bridging posthumanist political theory and feminist literary memory to ask, in a wrongful world, what does it mean to keep speaking when you are already counted among the living dead?
